item.title: Musings 3: Diversity
item.description:
• When it comes to today's colleges and their love for buzz phrases such as "fostering diversity" is truly deceitful and misleading. We should call them for what they truly are. Diversity in college is simply a reverse form of racism, where you value/devalue students based on the color of their skin. Asian students benefit the least in terms of college entrance. Blacks and Hispanics benefit the most. It's affirmative action based on your phenotype, and this would be the very type of bias, whether well-intentioned or not, Dr. Martin Luther King would have been against. What did Dr. King say? "I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin, but by the content of their character." He would be turning in his grave, if he saw what was going on in college campuses' crazy wave of intersectionality and pseudo-diversity. Call spade and spade. Diversity on campus is an action based on race, and it's harmful. Try diversity of thought for a change.

  • In this very well written article by professor of philosophy, Paul Russell at University of British Columbia, he makes a distinction between religious identity and human identity (which are phenotypes such as race, gender, sexual orientation that you have no control over).

  • This all goes in hand with the theme of regressive left intersectionality.

  • I truly believe that we have made so much progress in social acceptance in the last couple decades, and we've been spoiled, living in the world's leading country in almost every facet of life, that we are becoming blind to the order of priority in terms of human rights. As Dave Rubin once said, we have become so tolerant that we have become tolerant of the intolerant. Hopefully, I can write more on this topic

item.title: Musings 2: Turing Tests
item.description:
• The world isn't beautiful or rosy. We trick ourselves to think it's beautiful to live on.

  • Can machines question their own existensialism? If a script is written to explicitly question their consciousness, is it really conscious? In preparation for the Turing test questions such as, "Are you human?", if a machine is explictly programmed to answer intelligently, in the eyes of the arbiter, how is this not a pass? Humans are deterministic exactly like machines. We just happen to be made of cells instead of plastic, and neurons have larger memory capacity than any man-made chips/storage so far.

  • If we are part of a simulation a la Nick Bostrom, is there a Turing test version for humans that is analogous to man-made machines? What sort or a level of intelligence test are we supposed to test in the eyes of the simulator (creation of the simulation)?

  • If humans can code explictly for machine to fail the Turing test, which is easily achieveable without any effort with the current state of artificial intelligence, it's possible humans are failing some version of Turing test at the moment in the simulation world. It's possible to hard-code some machine so that it doesn't ask certain questions, thus intentionally failing certain tests. Similar argument could be made on humans in the simulation world. Humans may have been hard-wired to evade certain existential questions or to fail higher-dimensional Turing test, all in the context of simulation/simulator.

item.title: Musings 1: Theistic Reasoning vs. Scientific Reasoning
item.description:
• Suppose you're teaching a math class, and you give a problem for students to solve. Students are expected to show all work for their calculations. In solving the problem, one student demonstrates lack of rudimentary understanding of algebra. There are endless number of mistakes in his work, and yet somehow he concludes with the correct answer. Another student demonstrates his thorough foundational knowledge in algebra by carefully showing work line by line, but a mistake in computation costs him the answer. Whose work should be credited as valid? In academia, there wouldn't be a question as to whose work should be accepted as true or not. This is the difference between theisitic reasoning vs. scientific reasoning. Of course I'm not saying that the theists even have the conclusion correct. So technically, thei work is wrong and the final solution most likely would be wrong as well.

  • I'm hesistant with acitivism in general. I worry about the slightest chance that I choose the wrong side of the history. Conviction should be hard to come by. A thorough examination of facts and conclusive choice of action are no trivial tasks. I also make sure no personal or emotional biases are involved in decision making for the very same reason from earlier. That's why it's hard to watch impassioned young students, whose sincerety I don't doubt, run around in mass-hysteria politcal activism. Mass hysteria is a real social phenomenon.

  • Postmodernism contributes to conflation of emotions with facts, and thus makes the politcal activism more temperamental than it was originally intended to be.

item.title: Topics that I will talk about on this blog
item.description:
These are philosophical topics that interest me, and that have nothing to do with my research:

  • Peter Singer's Drowning Child Argument in Wars
  • Cognitive Dissonance
  • Jouranlism vs. Activism (Case Study: Regressive Left)
  • Simulation Hypothesis of Nick Bostrom, and Martin Savage's Setup
  • Ontological Proofs
  • Mortality of my cat, Baxter
